planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; Appaloosa bean is a distinctive bush dry bean with dramatic black and white spotted coloring reminiscent of an Appaloosa horse — a visually striking heirloom that produces excellent-flavored, creamy-textured beans for baking, soups, and slow-cooking.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in full sun; direct seeding is preferred as beans do not transplant well. Appaloosa bean is an heirloom dry bean named for its dramatic black and white spotted coloring that closely resembles the distinctive coat pattern of the Appaloosa horse breed; the irregular black speckles and splashes on creamy white background make each bean unique and the dried beans are among the most visually striking of all heirloom bean varieties, remaining attractive even after cooking. The flavor is rich and earthy with a creamy texture similar to pinto beans but with more character; they are excellent for baked beans, bean soups, and slow-cooked dishes. Can be eaten as a snap bean when pods are young and tender; most commonly grown for dry use at 85–100 days to full maturity. Drought-tolerant once established; performs well in Gulf Coast spring planting with harvest before summer heat peaks.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost and provide a trellis or pole support; Purple Podded Pole bean is one of the most visually dramatic vegetable plants available — producing vines covered in deep purple pods that stand out brilliantly against the green foliage, though the pods turn standard green when cooked; a beautiful edible ornamental for trellises and arbors.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost at base of a trellis, poles, or fence that will support 6–8 foot vines; thin to 4–6 inches apart in rows 24–30 inches apart. Purple Podded Pole bean is an old American heirloom snap bean with the dramatic characteristic of deep purple pods that develop against the green foliage — a striking visual contrast in the garden that makes this one of the most ornamentally interesting of all vegetable plants. The long (6–8 inch), round-sectioned pods are deep maroon-purple when raw and suitable for fresh eating or canning; however, the anthocyanin purple pigment is water-soluble and the pods turn standard green when cooked in water, a characteristic that surprises many gardeners. The flavor is excellent, similar to other pole snap beans; the raw purple pods are also edible in salads where the color can be appreciated. Very productive and continuous bearing through summer; the purple color also makes the beans somewhat easier to spot for harvesting against the green foliage.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; Pencil Pod Black Wax is an 1880s heirloom yellow wax (golden) bush snap bean, producing round, cylindrical (pencil-shaped), golden-yellow pods with black seeds — one of the classic American wax beans prized for its crisp texture, mild flavor, and beautiful color that holds when canned or cooked.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep in rows 18–24 inches apart after last frost; succession sow every 2–3 weeks for continuous harvest through spring. Pencil Pod Black Wax is an American heirloom wax bean introduced around 1880, named for its distinctive round, cylindrical pods (resembling a pencil in shape — round rather than flat) and black seeds; the 'wax' designation refers to the golden-yellow color of the pods, which are more ornamentally attractive than standard green beans and have a somewhat different, milder flavor. The pods are harvested at 4–6 inches when crisp and tender; like all wax beans, the yellow color holds through cooking and canning better than green beans, making the finished product more visually appealing in jars. Black Wax beans were a standard American commercial canning variety in the late 19th and early 20th centuries; now maintained by heirloom seed companies. One of the most productive and reliable early wax beans for succession planting.

common_name: King of the Garden Lima Bean

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; King of the Garden is one of the oldest and most celebrated American pole lima beans, introduced by Burpee in 1883 and prized for its very large, flat, creamy-white seeds with exceptional buttery lima flavor — the archetype of the 'large lima' bean and ideal for Gulf Coast and Southern gardens where the long season allows full development.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost with soil above 65°F; provide poles or strong trellising 6–8 feet tall for support. King of the Garden is a classic American pole lima bean introduced by W. Atlee Burpee in 1883 and considered for much of its history the finest large-seeded lima bean available; the plants are vigorous, 6–8 foot climbers that produce long, broad, flat pods with 4–6 very large (up to 1 inch), flat, creamy-white bean seeds with an extraordinarily rich, buttery, full lima bean flavor superior to most modern bush varieties. The large seeds require a relatively long, warm season to develop fully (85–95 days from seed); in Gulf Coast and Southern gardens the long growing season makes King of the Garden ideal for both spring and fall plantings. Excellent for fresh green lima use (harvested at the flat-but-not-yet-wrinkled stage when seeds are fully developed), freezing, and drying. One of the foundational American heirloom vegetables.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost at the base of sturdy support 6–8 feet tall; Wren's Egg is one of the oldest documented American bean varieties, known since the early 19th century, producing speckled cream and red or buff and maroon pods and seeds that strongly resemble a wren's speckled egg — grown as both a snap bean and a dry bean for its excellent flavor and historical significance.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost; provide trellis or poles 5–7 feet tall for support; vines twine naturally. Wren's Egg (also called 'Bird Egg', 'Speckled Bird Egg', or 'Speckled Cranberry Pole') is one of the oldest heirloom pole beans in continuous American cultivation, documented in seed catalogs and garden records since at least the early 1800s and traditionally maintained in New England and mid-Atlantic home gardens; it is named for the distinctive speckled coloring of both the pods and the dried seeds, which closely resemble the delicate speckled pattern of a wren's egg — a mix of cream or buff background with irregular red-brown, maroon, or rose speckles. The flat pods can be harvested young (45–60 days) as a snap bean or left to mature fully (75–90 days) for dry bean use. As a dry bean, Wren's Egg produces plump, oval seeds with excellent, rich flavor for baked beans and soups. The pods become increasingly stringy as they mature; harvest snap pods at 4–6 inches for best eating quality.
